---
title: "BeautifulSoup Extraction Patterns"
subtitle: "Selectors, nested HTML, attributes, and repeated records"
author: "methodsNET course"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: false
  pdf:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
jupyter: python3
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Why This Notebook Exists

The first scraping walkthrough uses a clean practice page. Real pages are often
messier: nested tags, multiple classes, attributes, tables, repeated cards, and
navigation mixed with content.

This notebook gives small, controlled examples.

```{python}
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup
import pandas as pd
```

# Example HTML

```{python}
html = """
<html>
  <body>
    <section id="results">
      <article class="result card promoted" data-id="p1">
        <h2><a href="/posts/p1">First post</a></h2>
        <p class="summary">This is the first summary.</p>
        <span class="author">Noelle</span>
        <ul class="tags">
          <li><a class="tag" href="/tags/api">api</a></li>
          <li><a class="tag" href="/tags/platforms">platforms</a></li>
        </ul>
      </article>

      <article class="result card" data-id="p2">
        <h2><a href="/posts/p2">Second post</a></h2>
        <p class="summary">This is the second summary.</p>
        <span class="author">Alex</span>
        <ul class="tags">
          <li><a class="tag" href="/tags/scraping">scraping</a></li>
        </ul>
      </article>
    </section>
  </body>
</html>
"""

soup = BeautifulSoup(html, "html.parser")
```

# `select_one()` vs `select()`

`select_one()` returns the first matching element.

```{python}
first_card = soup.select_one(".result")
print(first_card["data-id"])
```

`select()` returns a list of all matching elements.

```{python}
cards = soup.select(".result")
print(len(cards))
```

# Multiple Classes

This selector:

```{python}
soup.select(".result.card")
```

means: select elements that have both class `result` and class `card`.

It does **not** mean a `.card` inside a `.result`.

```{python}
for card in soup.select(".result.card"):
    print(card["data-id"], card.get("class"))
```

This selector:

```{python}
soup.select(".result .tag")
```

means: find `.tag` elements somewhere inside `.result` elements.

```{python}
for tag in soup.select(".result .tag"):
    print(tag.get_text(strip=True))
```

# Attributes

HTML attributes store information inside tags.

```{python}
first_link = soup.select_one("h2 a")

print(first_link.get_text(strip=True))
print(first_link["href"])
```

The `data-id` attribute is not visible text, but it may be a useful identifier.

```{python}
for card in soup.select(".result.card"):
    print(card["data-id"])
```

# Repeated Record Extraction

The safest pattern is:

1. select repeated record containers
2. loop over containers
3. select fields inside each container

```{python}
rows = []

for card in soup.select(".result.card"):
    title_link = card.select_one("h2 a")
    summary = card.select_one(".summary")
    author = card.select_one(".author")
    tag_links = card.select(".tags a.tag")

    rows.append(
        {
            "post_id": card.get("data-id"),
            "title": title_link.get_text(" ", strip=True) if title_link else None,
            "url": title_link.get("href") if title_link else None,
            "summary": summary.get_text(" ", strip=True) if summary else None,
            "author": author.get_text(" ", strip=True) if author else None,
            "tags": [tag.get_text(" ", strip=True) for tag in tag_links],
        }
    )

df = pd.DataFrame(rows)
df
```

# Direct Children vs Descendants

A space means "descendant at any depth".

```{python}
print([x.name for x in soup.select("#results a")])
```

The `>` symbol means "direct child".

```{python}
print([x.name for x in soup.select("#results > article")])
```

# Exercise

Using the example HTML:

1. Extract one row per article.
2. Add a Boolean column `is_promoted`.
3. Create a separate table with one row per article-tag pair.
4. Explain why `.result.card` and `.result .card` are different.

# Key Takeaway

Selectors are small, but they encode important assumptions about page structure.

Always inspect one record before looping over all records.
